title: Unrecoverable Errors 💥
description: When things go so wrong, the only option is to panic.

slides.title: Two Types of Errors 🚥
slides.content:
Rust groups errors into two major categories:

1. **Recoverable Errors**: Like 'file not found'. You want to gracefully report this to the user and let them try again.
2. **Unrecoverable Errors**: Like accessing an array out of bounds. This is a fatal logic bug, and the program must stop instantly.

slides.title: Time to Panic! 🚨

quizData.question: What is the primary function of the `panic!` macro?

options.text: To pause the execution for a few seconds
To immediately and safely halt the entire program because a fatal bug has occurred.
To warn the user but keep running.

quizData.explanation: Panic is exclusively reserved for catastrophic fatal logic errors that should terminate the program to prevent data corruption.

slides.title: Implicit Panics 🙈
slides.content:
You don't always have to write `panic!` yourself. Rust will do it for you to protect you from undefined behavior.

```rust
let v = vec![1, 2, 3];
v[99]; // PANIC!
```
In C++, this would silently read random garbage computer memory. In Rust, it securely panics and halts to protect your system.

quizData.question: Rust prevents completely random memory-read exploits safely and automatically by triggering a fatal `panic!` if you access out of bounds.
quizData.explanation: Bound-checking is heavily integrated natively to prevent undefined behavior and hackers exploiting memory leaks.
